era_context: The year 1500 marks the transition from the Middle Ages to the Early Modern period, characterized by the 'Age of Discovery' and the consolidation of powerful gunpowder empires. European maritime expansion is accelerating, the Italian Renaissance is at its peak, and major land-based powers like the Ming Dynasty, the Ottoman Empire, and the burgeoning Safavid and Mughal precursors are shaping Eurasia.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Interior North America (pre-contact records are archaeological/oral)
Central and Southern Africa (limited contemporary written records outside of Islamic influence zones)
Oceania (primarily oral tradition and archaeological dating)

events.title: Portuguese Discovery of Brazil
events.description: A fleet led by Pedro Álvares Cabral, originally destined for India, swung far west into the Atlantic and sighted the coast of South America. Cabral claimed the land for the Portuguese Crown, naming it 'Ilha de Vera Cruz' (Island of the True Cross). This event integrated South America into the Portuguese maritime empire and initiated the colonial history of Brazil.

sources.name: Letter of Pêro Vaz de Caminha

events.certainty_note: The date of April 22, 1500, is precisely documented in contemporary reports to King Manuel I.

events.title: Ismail I captures Tabriz and founds Safavid Dynasty
events.description: The young leader of the Safaviyya Sufi order, Ismail I, defeated the Aq Qoyunlu (White Sheep Turkmen) and captured Tabriz. He declared himself Shah of Persia, establishing the Safavid Dynasty and proclaiming Twelver Shi'ism as the official state religion. This act fundamentally reshaped the religious and political landscape of the Middle East, creating a lasting sectarian divide with the Sunni Ottoman Empire.

sources.name: Tarikh-i Alam-ara-yi Abbasi (History of Shah Abbas)

events.certainty_note: While the capture of Tabriz is certain, some sources place the formal coronation in 1501; 1500 marks the decisive military campaign.

events.title: Battle of Vedrosha
events.region: Eastern Europe (Grand Duchy of Lithuania / Muscovy)
events.description: A major engagement in the Muscovite–Lithuanian Wars where the forces of the Grand Duchy of Moscow, led by Prince Daniil Shchenya, decisively defeated the Lithuanian army. The victory allowed Ivan III (the Great) to consolidate control over significant territories in modern-day Belarus and Ukraine. This shifted the balance of power in Eastern Europe toward Moscow.
events.certainty_note: Well-documented in both Russian and Lithuanian chronicles of the period.

events.title: Diogo Dias discovers Madagascar
events.description: Part of Cabral's fleet, Diogo Dias became separated during a storm and sighted an island he named São Lourenço, known today as Madagascar. This was the first European contact with the island, though it had long been part of Austronesian and Arab trade networks. The discovery opened the way for future Portuguese attempts to establish outposts on the island.

sources.name: Decadas da Asia by João de Barros

events.certainty_note: The separation of Dias from Cabral's fleet is a well-recorded maritime event.

events.title: Fall of the Emirate of Granada's last resistance (Alpujarras Rebellion)
events.description: Following the 1492 fall of Granada, forced conversions of Muslims (Mudéjars) led to a massive uprising in the Alpujarras mountains. In 1500, the Catholic Monarchs suppressed the rebellion with extreme force. This led to the Pragmatic of 1502, effectively ending legal Islam in Castile and forcing Muslims to choose between exile or baptism.
events.certainty_note: The military suppression and subsequent decrees are well-preserved in Spanish state archives.

events.title: Expansion of the Mexica (Aztec) Empire under Ahuitzotl
events.description: By 1500, the Aztec Empire reached its greatest territorial extent under the Huey Tlatoani Ahuitzotl. This year saw the completion of major public works in Tenochtitlan, including the Acuecuexatl aqueduct, which unfortunately caused a major flood in the city. The empire's reach now extended from the Pacific to the Gulf Coast.
events.certainty_note: Dating is based on the Aztec calendar cycles correlated with the Gregorian calendar; the flood of 1500 is a specific landmark event.

disconfirming_evidence: While 1500 is traditionally cited as the 'discovery' of Brazil by Cabral, some historians argue for earlier, clandestine Portuguese voyages (e.g., Duarte Pacheco Pereira in 1498) or Spanish sightings (Vicente Yáñez Pinzón earlier in 1500) that were suppressed for diplomatic reasons related to the Treaty of Tordesillas. Additionally, the 'founding' of the Safavid Empire is often debated between 1501 (coronation) and 1500 (military victory), with 1500 representing the transition of power rather than the formal statehood.
historiographic_note: The record for 1500 is heavily influenced by the 'Age of Discovery' narrative, which prioritizes European maritime logs and state-sponsored chronicles. This creates a bias where the internal histories of the Americas and Africa are often viewed through the lens of first contact rather than their own internal developments. However, the high literacy of the Renaissance and the bureaucratic nature of the Ming and Ottoman empires provide a relatively stable global chronology.

graph_edges.note: The Treaty of Tordesillas (1494) set the longitudinal line that allowed Portugal to claim Brazil in 1500.
The rise of the Safavids led directly to the Battle of Chaldiran against the Ottomans.
